requirements.background: Ensures consistency across the entire QGSD codebase by purging all legacy model-based references and replacing them with slot names. Without this, mixed terminology in different tools (some showing slot names, others showing old names) would confuse users about whether they are the same entity. Completes the naming migration and prevents gradual regression back to old names.

requirements.text: `mcp-status`, `mcp-set-model`, `mcp-update`, `mcp-restart` accept and display slot names correctly
requirements.background: Establishes slot-aware UX in the main operational commands that users interact with daily. These commands must accept and display slot names consistently so users can manage their quorum using the unified naming convention established in SLOT-01. Closes the gap between conceptual (slot names) and operational (user commands).

requirements.text: Stop hook formalized in `QGSDStopHook.tla` + `MCStopHook.cfg`, TLC verifies safety + liveness
requirements.background: Formalizes the stop hook (which blocks planning responses without quorum consent) in TLA+ with its own model checker config (MCStopHook.cfg). TLC verifies that the stop hook satisfies safety (never allows unvetted responses) and liveness (quorum consent eventually arrives). Foundation for SPEC-02 and SPEC-03 which extend this spec composition.

requirements.text: `QGSDOscillation.tla` audited against `qgsd-circuit-breaker.js` — no drift detected
requirements.background: Audits the oscillation circuit breaker (run-collapse algorithm in qgsd-circuit-breaker.js) against its TLA+ formalization (QGSDOscillation.tla). No drift detected means the implementation faithfully executes the formal model. Provides confidence that LOOP-02's spec regeneration won't create oscillation edge cases.

requirements.text: Quorum composition verified in `quorum-composition.als` — 3 composition rules hold
requirements.background: Verifies in Alloy that quorum composition rules hold: (1) at least 2 of 5 slots must agree, (2) no single slot dominates consensus, (3) slot selection is deterministic. These three rules are verified through formal constraint satisfaction. Supports LOOP-01 and SIG-04 by proving quorum semantics are sound.

requirements.text: `generate-phase-spec.cjs` translates PLAN.md `must_haves: truths:` to TLA+ PROPERTY stubs
requirements.background: Auto-generates TLA+ PROPERTY stubs from PLAN.md truth declarations. Bridges planning language (must_haves/truths) and formal verification language (TLA+ PROPERTY). Enables PLAN-02 to verify plans by providing the formal assertion that planning intends. Output feeds directly into PLAN-02's iterative TLC loop.

requirements.text: audit-milestone updates STATE.md "Stopped at" and "Current Position" fields with the audit result (passed / gaps_found / tech_debt) after writing the MILESTONE-AUDIT.md artifact
requirements.background: Tracks milestone audit outcomes by updating STATE.md "Stopped at" and "Current Position" fields with results (passed / gaps_found / tech_debt). Provides persistent state across context resets and enables resume-work to recover at the exact point where audit stopped. Works with LOOP-03 to ensure audit results drive visibility and recovery logic.

requirements.text: State schema: `{ active, file_set[], activated_at, commit_window_snapshot[] }` — captures what triggered the breaker
requirements.background: State schema captures what triggered the breaker: { active, file_set[], activated_at, commit_window_snapshot[] }. This complete snapshot allows recovery tools and the user to understand exactly which files are oscillating and when the breaker activated.

requirements.text: Hook reads existing state first — if active, applies enforcement immediately without re-running git log detection
requirements.background: Hook reads existing state first — if active, apply enforcement immediately without re-running git log. This optimization avoids redundant git queries when the breaker is already active, improving performance and reducing system load.

requirements.text: State file created silently if absent; failure to write logs to stderr but never blocks execution
requirements.background: State file is created silently if absent, and write failures log to stderr but never block execution. This defensive design prevents the circuit breaker from stopping all work due to file system issues — it degrades gracefully.

requirements.text: All 4 Gen1 MCP server repos (claude, codex, copilot, openhands) ported to Gen2 per-tool architecture (`*.tool.ts` + `registry.ts`) with Gen1 files (`definitions.ts`, `handlers.ts`) removed — all repos on main branch
requirements.background: Standardizes MCP server implementation across four Gen1 repos (claude, codex, copilot, openhands) by adopting the Gen2 per-tool architecture. This modernization ensures all quorum agents use consistent tool registration and reduces maintenance burden. Necessary foundation before quorum composition (COMP) and slot management (SLOT) can work reliably.

requirements.text: gemini-mcp-server npm package name is unscoped (`gemini-mcp-server`, not `@tuannvm/gemini-mcp-server`) — `~/.claude.json` mcpServers["gemini-cli"].args reflects the unscoped name
requirements.background: Ensures the gemini-mcp-server npm package uses an unscoped name, consistent with npm registry best practices. The ~/.claude.json entry must reference the correct package name; mismatched names cause installation and startup failures. Related to standardization of all provider packages.

requirements.text: Stop hook reads transcript JSONL for tool_use entries matching configured quorum model names
requirements.background: STOP hook is the foundational enforcement gate that prevents planning responses without quorum. This requirement establishes that the hook must inspect the actual transcript JSONL for evidence of quorum tool invocations — reading tool_use entries is the authoritative, reliable method to verify that multi-model deliberation actually occurred. This is foundational to the entire quorum enforcement architecture.

requirements.text: Stop hook checks `stop_hook_active` flag first — if true, exits 0 immediately (infinite loop prevention)
requirements.background: Prevents infinite loops where the Stop hook itself might trigger on subsequent Claude responses. The stop_hook_active flag is a simple state guard that allows the hook to exit early if already executing, ensuring it cannot recurse and block itself indefinitely. This is a safety mechanism essential to hook stability.

requirements.text: Stop hook checks `hook_event_name` — if `SubagentStop`, exits 0 immediately (subagent exclusion)
requirements.background: Excludes subagent invocations from quorum verification. Subagents operate in constrained contexts where they cannot invoke quorum tools themselves, so requiring full quorum at the subagent boundary would deadlock. This requirement ensures hooks only apply to top-level Claude responses.

requirements.text: Stop hook scopes transcript search to current turn only (lines since last user message boundary) — survives context compaction
requirements.background: Scopes quorum verification to the current turn only, meaning only the user message and its corresponding Claude response are checked for quorum evidence. This survives context compaction because the scope is defined by message boundaries, not absolute line numbers. Ensures quorum checks remain valid even after context is summarized.

requirements.text: Stop hook reads transcript JSONL as the authoritative source of quorum evidence — no fast-path pre-check (design decision: last_assistant_message substring matching is not a reliable signal; JSONL parse is synchronous and correct for all transcript sizes)
requirements.background: Establishes JSONL parsing as the authoritative source rather than heuristics like substring matching in last_assistant_message. This design decision prioritizes correctness over speed — parsing is synchronous and accurate regardless of transcript size, preventing false negatives where quorum evidence exists but heuristics miss it.

requirements.text: Stop hook verifies quorum only when a configured planning command was issued in the current turn (scope filtering)
requirements.background: Quorum is only required when a planning command was actually issued in the current turn. This scope filtering prevents false blocks on non-planning responses (e.g., answering casual questions). The hook checks if the user message matches the configured quorum_commands list before enforcing verification.

requirements.text: Stop hook blocks with `{"decision": "block", "reason": "..."}` when quorum is missing — reason includes exact tool names and instructions
requirements.background: Defines the block response format for blocking responses. Using a structured decision field with reason text allows Claude Code to display clear, actionable feedback to the user. The reason must include tool names so Claude knows exactly what to invoke.

requirements.text: Block reason message format: "QUORUM REQUIRED: Before completing this /qgsd:[command] response, call [tool1], [tool2], [tool3] with your current plan. Present their responses, then deliver your final output."
requirements.background: Specifies the exact message format for blocks, ensuring consistent UX and clear instructions. The message pattern 'QUORUM REQUIRED: ... call [tool1], [tool2], [tool3] ... Present their responses, then deliver your final output' is explicit enough that Claude can understand what to do without additional context.

requirements.text: Stop hook passes (exits 0, no decision field) when quorum evidence found or no planning command in scope
requirements.background: Defines the success case — the hook passes (exits cleanly with no decision field) when quorum evidence is found in transcript or when no planning command triggered in the current turn. This allows planning responses with full quorum to complete without further blocking.

requirements.text: QGSD ships as separate npm package (`qgsd` or `get-shit-done-quorum`) that wraps GSD
requirements.background: QGSD is a separate npm package that wraps GSD. This separation allows independent versioning and prevents QGSD changes from affecting GSD installations. QGSD can be installed anywhere GSD is installed without modifying GSD's code.

requirements.text: When GSD releases a new planning command, QGSD releases a patch update adding the command to the default `quorum_commands` list
requirements.background: When GSD releases new planning commands, QGSD patches its quorum_commands list. This keeps quorum requirements synchronized with GSD's evolving feature set, preventing new commands from bypassing quorum enforcement.

requirements.text: QGSD changelog explicitly tracks which GSD version it is compatible with
requirements.background: QGSD changelog tracks compatible GSD versions. Users can see which GSD version their QGSD works with, aiding troubleshooting when versions drift.

requirements.text: No QGSD code modifies any GSD source files — all additions are in separate files (`hooks/qgsd-stop.js`, `hooks/qgsd-prompt.js`, `bin/qgsd-install.js`)
requirements.background: QGSD never modifies GSD source files — all additions are in separate files (hooks/qgsd-stop.js, hooks/qgsd-prompt.js, bin/qgsd-install.js). Zero-coupling is a hard constraint that allows QGSD to be uninstalled cleanly without contaminating GSD.

requirements.text: `bin/generate-triage-bundle.cjs` reads `check-results.ndjson` and writes `formal/diff-report.md` (per-check delta from last run) and `formal/suspects.md` (checks with `result=fail` or `triage_tags` set)
requirements.background: This requirement creates a triage bundle tool that generates two artifacts from check-results.ndjson: formal/diff-report.md (per-check deltas from the last run) and formal/suspects.md (checks with result=fail or triage_tags set). It solves the analysis problem where hundreds of check results are hard to navigate without a summary. By generating a diff report, it shows what changed between runs (helping identify flaky checks or regressions). By flagging suspects, it highlights priority areas for human investigation. This pairs with TRIAGE-02 to make formal verification results actionable.

requirements.text: `run-formal-verify.cjs` calls `generate-triage-bundle.cjs` as the final step after all checks complete
requirements.background: This requirement integrates triage bundle generation as the final step in run-formal-verify.cjs, ensuring diff-report.md and suspects.md are always generated alongside check-results.ndjson. It solves the workflow integration problem where triage artifacts might be forgotten or generated out-of-sync. By making triage generation automatic and mandatory, it ensures every formal verification run produces actionable summaries. This pairs with TRIAGE-01 to embed triage analysis into the core verification pipeline.

requirements.text: All FV checkers append normalized JSON to `check-results.ndjson` — `{ tool, check_id, result, detail, ts }`
requirements.background: v0.19 established a unified output format (check-results.ndjson) to eliminate tool stdout parsing fragility. Each formal verification checker writes structured JSON entries ({tool, check_id, result, detail, ts}) to a single canonical file, enabling deterministic CI result detection and preventing race conditions where stdout was lost or interleaved. This is the foundation for all downstream verification enforcement.

requirements.text: `run-formal-verify.cjs` generates `check-results.ndjson` as canonical output artifact
requirements.background: The orchestrator run-formal-verify.cjs needed to generate the check-results.ndjson artifact itself, not just collect it from tools. This ensures artifact location is always predictable and enables CI enforcement to locate and parse results reliably without tool-specific path conventions, solving the problem where verification results were previously scattered across stderr/stdout.

requirements.text: Triage bundle reads from `check-results.ndjson`, not tool stdout; CI enforcement steps run inside orchestrator before summary read (v0.19-11 timing fix)
requirements.background: The triage bundle was reading tool stdout directly, missing failures and creating a race between parallel CI enforcement steps and summary report generation. v0.19-11 fixed timing by requiring CI enforcement steps to run inside the orchestrator BEFORE the summary reads the artifact, guaranteeing results are finalized before triage interpretation.

requirements.text: CI step exits non-zero when any `result=fail` entry exists in `check-results.ndjson`
requirements.background: CI pipelines must fail fast when any formal verification check fails. This requirement ensures check-results.ndjson is the authoritative decision point—the orchestrator exits non-zero if any entry has result=fail, eliminating the possibility of failed checks being overlooked due to tool-specific error propagation logic.

requirements.text: A UPPAAL timed automaton model (`formal/uppaal/quorum-races.xml`) captures the concurrency structure of the quorum protocol — specifically when concurrent slot responses and timeout expirations fire relative to each other. The model uses `runtime_ms` bounds from `check-results.ndjson` as empirical timing constraints (clock guards and invariants), not hardcoded constants.
requirements.background: This requirement creates a UPPAAL timed automaton model (formal/uppaal/quorum-races.xml) that formalizes the concurrency structure of the quorum protocol, specifically timing interactions between concurrent slot responses and timeout expirations. It solves the timing analysis problem where TLA+ safety/liveness properties don't quantify over real-time constraints. By capturing clock guards and invariant timing bounds derived from empirical runtime_ms data in check-results.ndjson, the model enables detection of race conditions that would manifest only under specific timing scenarios. This is a new formalism complementing TLA+.

requirements.text: `bin/run-uppaal.cjs` executes the UPPAAL model checker (verifyta CLI) against `quorum-races.xml` and writes a check result to `check-results.ndjson` using the v2.1 schema (SCHEMA-01 prerequisite). The STEPS entry `uppaal:quorum-races` is added to `run-formal-verify.cjs`.
requirements.background: This requirement operationalizes the UPPAAL model by creating bin/run-uppaal.cjs that executes the verifyta model checker against quorum-races.xml and writes results to check-results.ndjson using v2.1 schema. It solves the integration problem where a new formalism must produce outputs compatible with the existing verification pipeline. By adding the uppaal:quorum-races STEPS entry in run-formal-verify.cjs, UPPAAL checking becomes part of the standard formal verification flow. This depends on SCHEMA-01 for output compatibility.

requirements.text: The model surfaces at least two critical measurement points as annotated properties: (a) the minimum inter-slot response gap that prevents a race condition, (b) the maximum timeout value for which the quorum can still reach consensus before the planning gate deadline.
requirements.background: This requirement ensures the UPPAAL model surfaces at least two critical measurement points: (a) the minimum inter-slot response gap that prevents race conditions, and (b) the maximum timeout value for consensus before the planning deadline. It solves the parameter sensitivity problem where the model must identify which timing parameters are critical for correctness. By annotating these as properties in the UPPAAL model, it enables the sensitivity analysis (SENS-01/02/03) to understand which timing parameters to sweep and what boundary values are safe. This connects timing analysis to parameter sensitivity.

requirements.text: UserPromptSubmit hook detects GSD planning commands via explicit allowlist regex match against prompt field
requirements.background: UserPromptSubmit hook proactively injects quorum instructions before Claude starts generating a response. This requirement establishes detection via allowlist regex — explicit matching of command patterns avoids false positives from casual mentions of 'plan-phase' in prose.

requirements.text: Allowlist contains exactly 6 commands: new-project, plan-phase, new-milestone, discuss-phase, verify-work, research-phase
requirements.background: Defines the exact 6 commands that require quorum: new-project, plan-phase, new-milestone, discuss-phase, verify-work, research-phase. This hardcoded list is the single source of truth for which planning workflows trigger quorum enforcement, enabling predictable behavior.

requirements.text: UserPromptSubmit hook injects quorum instructions via `hookSpecificOutput.additionalContext` (not systemMessage — goes into Claude's context window)
requirements.background: Injects quorum instructions via additionalContext rather than systemMessage because additionalContext goes into Claude's regular context window where it can be quoted and reasoned about. This approach is more transparent and allows the user to see what instructions were injected.

requirements.text: Injected context names the exact MCP tools to call and instructs Claude to present model responses before delivering final output
requirements.background: The injected context must explicitly name the MCP tools to call (e.g., 'mcp__codex-1__codex') and instruct Claude to 'present their responses, then deliver your final output'. This ensures Claude knows which tools are required and in what order to present results.

requirements.text: UserPromptSubmit hook never fires on execute-phase or other non-planning commands
requirements.background: Ensures UserPromptSubmit only fires on planning commands, not on non-planning commands like execute-phase or casual interactions. This prevents unnecessary quorum injection on all responses and keeps the overhead low for non-planning work.

requirements.text: `qgsd-verifier` agent runs `run-formal-verify` after implementation and includes `check-results.ndjson` digest in `VERIFICATION.md`
requirements.background: This requirement adds a qgsd-verifier agent that runs the full formal verification suite (all 23 checks) after implementation phases complete. It solves the post-hoc validation problem where implementations might diverge from their intended specifications without anyone noticing. By running run-formal-verify and including the check-results.ndjson digest in VERIFICATION.md, it provides evidence that the implemented code matches the formal model. This pairs with VERIFY-02 to make formal verification results part of the phase delivery artifact.

requirements.text: `VERIFICATION.md` template gains a `## Formal Verification` section summarizing pass/fail/warn counts per formalism (tla, alloy, prism, ci)
requirements.background: This requirement extends the VERIFICATION.md template with a structured Formal Verification section that summarizes pass/fail/warn counts per formalism (TLA+, Alloy, PRISM, CI). It solves the reporting problem where formal verification results exist but aren't summarized for human review. By providing per-formalism breakdowns, it enables readers to quickly assess which formalisms passed, which had warnings, and which failed, supporting decision-making about whether to ship the phase. This pairs with VERIFY-01 to embed formal evidence into the delivery process.

requirements.text: `/qgsd:health` surfaces W008 warning when a quorum slot has ≥3 failures recorded in `quorum-failures.json`
requirements.background: This requirement surfaces recurring quorum slot failures in the health output by adding a Check 9 that reads `.planning/quorum-failures.json` and emits W008 warnings for any slot with 3+ failures. This is critical for operator visibility because quorum failures indicate that one or more AI providers is experiencing systematic issues (timeouts, authentication failures, etc.), which impacts the reliability of multi-model consensus operations. Without surfacing these failures in routine health checks, operators would have to manually inspect the quorum-failures.json file; integrating the check into `/qgsd:health` makes failure detection automatic and actionable alongside other health indicators.

requirements.text: User can run `/qgsd:mcp-setup` to start the MCP configuration wizard
requirements.background: Foundation for the entire MCP configuration system. This slash command entry point must exist before any configuration can happen. It serves as the single interface for users to set up, modify, or inspect their quorum agent roster, avoiding the need for manual JSON editing. Related to WIZ-02 through WIZ-05 which define the UX flow and interactions within the wizard.

requirements.text: First run (no configured agents) presents a guided linear onboarding flow step by step
requirements.background: Enables first-time users to configure quorum agents without prior knowledge of QGSD architecture. The guided linear flow reduces friction and ensures all required fields (name, provider, model, key) are collected before creating agents. Works with AGENT-01 and INST-01 to ensure new installations are not left unconfigured.

requirements.text: Re-run shows the current agent roster as a navigable menu
requirements.background: Allows experienced users to re-run the wizard and see their existing agent configuration rather than starting from scratch. This menu-based approach provides quick access to modify or add agents. Pairs with WIZ-04 to give users visibility into agent status before making changes.

requirements.text: Each agent in the menu shows current model, provider, and key status (present/missing)
requirements.background: Critical for user awareness when managing agents. By displaying the model, provider, and key status inline, users can quickly identify which agents are ready vs. incomplete. This information guides decisions about which agents to modify or delete, connecting provider swap (PROV) and key management (KEY) features.

requirements.text: User confirms before changes are applied; wizard restarts affected agents after apply
requirements.background: Safety guardrail that prevents accidental agent configuration changes. The confirmation step before apply, combined with automatic restart of affected agents, ensures changes take effect reliably. Required for trustworthy key rotation (KEY) and provider swap (PROV) operations.

requirements.text: `/qgsd:mcp-setup` re-run menu includes an "Edit Quorum Composition" option
requirements.background: Adds an "Edit Quorum Composition" menu option to the mcp-setup wizard so users can modify `quorum.active` without leaving the wizard. Solves the discovery problem — users may not know that composition editing exists if it's only documented. Integrates composition management into the primary wizard flow.

requirements.text: Composition screen shows all discovered slots with on/off toggle for `quorum.active` inclusion
requirements.background: Provides visual representation of all discovered slots with on/off toggles for `quorum.active` inclusion. This UI directly manipulates COMP-01's configuration. Prevents users from having to edit JSON manually or remember slot names, reducing configuration errors and friction.

requirements.text: User can add a new slot for any family (claude, copilot, opencode, codex-cli, gemini-cli) from within the wizard, which writes the new entry to `~/.claude.json` and triggers restart
requirements.background: Enables adding a new slot for any family directly from within the wizard, writing to `~/.claude.json` and triggering restart. This is the user-facing entry point for MULTI-01/MULTI-02 functionality. Allows users to expand their quorum without leaving the guided setup flow or restarting Claude Code externally.
